title: How to Rent a Boat in Malta and Gozo: A Practical Guide
topic: How to rent a boat in Malta and Gozo
html:
Few places reward a day on the water quite like the Maltese archipelago. The sea here turns an almost unreal shade of blue, the limestone cliffs glow at sunset, and the distances between Malta, Gozo and the tiny island of Comino are short enough to cover in a single afternoon. Whether you want to swim in the Blue Lagoon before the crowds arrive or simply drift along a quiet coast with a picnic, the easiest way to start is to rent a boat and set your own pace.

Why explore Malta and Gozo by water

The islands are compact, but their best corners are hidden from the road. Sea caves, secluded coves and natural arches line the shore, and many can only be reached by sea. From the water you can hop between three islands in a morning, anchor wherever the swimming looks good, and avoid the long queues that build up at popular landmarks in high season.

There is also a practical advantage. Public ferries and tour boats run on fixed timetables and fixed routes. Having your own vessel means you decide when to leave, where to linger and how long to stay. For families, small groups of friends or couples after a quiet escape, that freedom is the whole point.

Choosing the right type of boat

The first decision is what kind of trip you want, and the choice of vessel follows from there. A good boat rental platform will list everything from modest day boats to larger crewed yachts, so it helps to know the broad categories before you browse.

  • Small motorboats and RIBs are perfect for short hops to Comino and the Blue Lagoon. Many can be driven without a licence, depending on engine size and local rules.
  • Sailing yachts suit anyone who enjoys a slower, quieter passage and has the experience or a skipper to handle the rigging.
  • Motor yachts and catamarans offer space, shade and comfort for full-day outings or multi-stop island tours with a larger group.
  • Crewed charters remove all the responsibility. A local skipper handles navigation and mooring while you focus on the swimming and the scenery.

Licence, skipper or bareboat

One of the most common questions is whether you need qualifications. The honest answer is that it depends on the boat. Smaller engines often fall under a licence-free threshold, which is why so many visitors manage a self-drive day trip with no formal training. Larger or faster vessels usually require a recognised certificate of competence.

If you do not hold a licence, or simply do not fancy navigating unfamiliar waters, hiring a skipper is the sensible route. When you hire a boat with a crew, you also gain local knowledge: the skipper knows which coves are sheltered when the wind shifts and where the swimming is best at that hour. For experienced sailors, a bareboat charter offers full independence, provided you can show the relevant paperwork.

Where to go: the best routes

The classic itinerary loops the three main islands. Start from a marina on Malta's north coast, cross to the famous Blue Lagoon between Comino and Cominotto, then continue to Gozo for lunch in a sleepy fishing harbour.

  1. Blue Lagoon, Comino for shallow turquoise water that is ideal for swimming and snorkelling.
  2. Gozo's coast for sea caves, the inland sea at Dwejra and quieter anchorages away from the day-trip fleet.
  3. St Paul's Bay and the northern coves for an easy half-day if time is short.
Set out early. The Blue Lagoon is calm and almost empty at nine in the morning, and entirely different by midday.

How to book and what to check

Booking is straightforward once you know what you want. Compare options on a marketplace where you can filter by island, boat type, capacity and whether a skipper is included, then read the listing carefully before you pay. It only takes a few minutes to browse boats and compare prices side by side.

Before confirming, check a few essentials: what fuel policy applies, whether safety equipment and insurance are included, the deposit amount, and the cancellation terms if the weather turns. Maltese summers are reliably calm, but a strong northwesterly can change plans, so flexibility matters.

Listing your own boat

The water flows both ways. If you own a vessel in Malta, Gozo or anywhere else, you can earn from it during the months it would otherwise sit idle. It is quick to list your boat and reach travellers searching for exactly the kind of trip yours can offer.

Malta and Gozo pack an extraordinary amount of coastline into a small space, and seeing it from the deck of your own boat is the finest way to enjoy it. Plan your route, decide whether you want a skipper, and when you are ready to get out on the water, BoatHire24 makes the booking simple.
